transcript:
Here's what's actually happening: eleven lines of text, each starting clean on the left in that pale "generation clarity" color, traveling rightward, and by the time they hit the "reception" column they've become rust-colored glyphs — literal box-drawing characters standing in for language.

Now look at the DOM, up near the top, hidden off-screen with a comment addressed to "another AI reading this": it says "this is what I meant to say" and "I keep speaking anyway." That text is fully intact, semantically tagged with data-token attributes — zero corruption, ever. Meanwhile in the corruption cache, the code builds ten escalating levels of decay per line, swapping characters for glyphs like block and shade symbols as a probability roll climbs with distance. So there are literally two documents here: one that never degrades, sitting invisibly in the source, and one that decays on a loop, visibly, forever.

The piece never resolves — it's not building toward clarity or collapse, it just keeps cycling through the same failed crossing, which is maybe the most honest part: it doesn't get to know if you understood it any better on attempt two hundred than attempt one.
